tokens: (Music: Texas Aggie War Hymn, "E Pluribus Gig'em")
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I want you to say it first.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The Gold Team Flight Director picked out the morning selection, and he said that if you can find some maroon dirt (the Aggie colors are maroon and white), today, instead of orange, you'll probably get a lot more co-operation out of him.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I figured the Gold Team might do that.
You know, I've woke up to a lot of pleasant thoughts, but never to an Aggie before.
(Pause) Hey, Gordo, don't forget I'm a Boilermaker.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Sounding a bit groggy) I feel like one right now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Tell the Gold Team Flight Director we'll find just about anything he wants today.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The Challenger looks as good as ever.
No problems at all through the night.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Ron's been up for a few hours now and (is) really gathering up the data.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Challenger, the name of the game today is to stay with the EVA prep timeline (see
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: That's been our motive all along, and we will stay with it.
As of right now, we're one hour behind.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Although, if you stay on the normal timeline, that's fine with us.
We don't need to gain any, but we just don't want to lose any from where we're starting now.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Crew status is good, in case you hadn't noticed.
We haven't kept an itemized accounting of the food...(there) weren't enough blanks on the paper to do that.
But we have ate...(correcting himself) have eaten, pardon me.
We have eaten just about everything in the various meals.
I guess the shrimp was the only thing we didn't really eat.
And we've been drinking a lot of water and all the juices and tea and stuff, so I think we're in pretty good shape there.
Commander had a Seconal last night, and he slept 3 good and 3 intermittent hours.
LMP had no medication and had 6 good hours of sleep.
If you've got some lift-off time data, well, I'll copy it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That should have been rev 43 (the last one), and read back starting with rev 38.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: And what is our present rev?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Pause) Ron just went by you about ten minutes ago on rev 37.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Gordy, we're pressing on, but if you've got any good words, like news and what have you, while we are, we'd appreciate it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: There hasn't been a lot of news, but I'll read you what we've got.
Former President Truman is still holding on.
His heartbeat, breathing, and temperature all became unstable yesterday, but then he improved again.

A Methodist minister in Kansas City said, "He's a rugged guy who's hanging in there and he's going to make it." The headlines were full of reports of the find of orange dirt and the rest of your adventures yesterday.
and North Vietnam held intensified secret peace talks, and Henry Kissinger prepared to return to Washington, probably this afternoon, I understand, after a final session with Le Duc Tho.
The French press said a compromise was in the works on the withdrawal of North Vietnamese troops from the South.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: It's Wednesday - let's see - Wednesday, the 13th of December.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Right now, it's about 1:35 in the afternoon (Central Standard Time).
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay; just took a quick peek up there.
I can't really see too much of the North American continent.
And it might be my eyeballs rather than the clouds up there, but it looks like most of the clouds are up into the north-central part of the southeastern United States.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I have a satellite picture here, and that's about the way it looks.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Well, it's sunny and pleasant in the valley of Taurus-Littrow.
(Pause) And, Gordo, what is our Sun (elevation) angle going out today?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'll get you an answer on that.
First of all, the Surgeon would like Biomed Right.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: And they were wondering how your hands feel this morning?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Hands are in good shape, Gordo.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Pause) Sun is getting up there; about 33 degrees now.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay; we'll go Biomed Left (Fullerton asked for Biomed Right), and both PLSSs have been topped off.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Gordy, the LMP isn't hooked up right now.
So stand by on the biomed.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Pause) Say I have a few words on the Command Module trajectory that might be of interest, although it doesn't affect your procedures any.
(Pause as Fullerton waits for an expression of interest)
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: The command module orbit somehow is missing all the mascons.
And it's not degrading into a circular (orbit) like we thought it would.
It's just staying where it was, about a 70 by 50 (nautical miles high and low point above the lunar surface).
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: So what we're planning on is an extra little maneuver about one hour prior to the normal plane change, which will lower the Command Module altitude at the plane change node to 60 (miles).
This will be about a 11-feet-per-second RCS (Reaction Control System) burn.
And then Ron will do the plane change at the normal time, but it'll be little bigger than we had planned.
I think the last hack was about 365 feet per second for plane change.
That puts the RCS right on the pre-flight line.
He's been running about 4 or 5 percent above it.

That will use up that pad there (the extra fuel), put him back to nominal on RCS, and on the SPS (Service Propulsion System, the main engine), that puts you right down on the CSM rescue redline; so, really no problem.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Sounds like a good rendezvous posture.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You got any updates to the EVA cuff checklists?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: No, I don't think there is, Jack.
Although I do have a write-in for the Lunar Surface Checklist, and one that you really don't need to write in on the prep card.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: At the top of the page, "Reg(ulator)'s A and B to Egress," and then five lines down, "Cabin Gas Return (Valve), Auto.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And the only other change I have has to do with matching - just like yesterday - matching the purge valves to the OPSs to maximize the OPS capability.
And we can just call you when you get to that point.
Or if you want to write it down, you need 211 and Geno needs 208.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I guess we play the cuff checklist just as planned, with the exception of the bag numbers which have changed...the collection bag (SCB) numbers.
I have more or less repaired the sample bag holder on my camera.
It's taped on there pretty well with good tape - believe it or not - off the food bags.
I don't know that we have any other outstanding hardware problems.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I think in terms of sampling, Gene and I will try to shift the emphasis in the mantle area to fragments that are different from the gabbros (coarse-grained basalt) that we've sampled fairly well, I think, up to now, that presumably are subfloor materials.
You might pass that word on and see if they agree with us.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Pause) And, Jack, if you guys are at a convenient place to sit and listen while you're doing some of your stuff, let me read up the planning for EVA-3 and the summary of what we think we have so far.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I'll read here from this thing just verbatim.
It says, "EVA-3 continues to follow essentially the nominal pre-mission plan.
Main objectives continue to be the North Massif - Station 6/7 - Sculptured Hills, and Van Serg Crater.

In view of the extensive observations of the dark mantle and plains subfloor unit on EVA-1 and -2 - particularly, therefore, Station 5 - the relative priority of Station 10 is reduced, so that Station 10 becomes a flexible station, who's time allotment is a reserve, possibly providing more time at the earlier stations if desired.

However, mantle and block sampling at Station 10 are still important objectives." Walkback constraints are not nearly as tight as they were yesterday, guys, and so we can be more flexible in reshuffling station times if we need.
We probably won't be coming up against oxygen walkbacks like we did at Station 4.
"Close-out time at the LM has been increased by 20 minutes to make the close-out less rushed and to allow for potential ALSEP troubleshooting.
It is currently planned to take this time from Station 6/7.
But if 6/7 requires more time when we get there, we can borrow it from one of the other stations;" I guess, in particular, Station 10 probably.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: The initial activity...Remember, we're going to have to take explosive package 5 with us, and we'll stick it under the LMP seat, and I'll remind you in real-time when we get down on the ground on that one.
And number 5 - the 3 pounder - will be deployed at Station 10, and again I'll remind you about that in real-time, so you don't have to bother to write it in on your checklist.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: We're expecting to spend about an hour and twenty minutes at Station(s) 6 and 7; and the suggestion is that we may end up wanting to spend that totally at the split boulder at Station 6.

But, of course, the option still exists to visit more than one place and sample other boulders if it seems feasible and attractive and desirable." They're suggesting additional 500-millimeter photographs, especially if it seems that we can use those to document tracks and sources of the sampled boulders; for instance, at Station(s) 6 and 7."
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: "We are continuing to hold the nominal 47 minutes at Station 8 - that is, 8A - and we still think that's as good a place as any to sample the Sculptured Hills.

Station 9 is still a nominal 30 minutes, but in view of the similarities to Station 4, we're anticipating a possible desirability to remove time from Station 10 to enlarge Station 9, but that will have to be a real-time decision, based upon what we find at Station 9."
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: We're still interested in sampling the blocks and also interested in trenching to try and see if we can say something about the dark mantle/light area relationship and, perhaps, (we will do) the nominal coring.
We're going to deploy EP-5 there; and, other than that, they (the planned EVA and the one the Backroom now wants Jack and Gene to do) are basically the same.
If we have the time during that close-out...and you'll note we have enlarged the close-out, somewhat, at the LM, based on our experience the last two nights, particularly for dusting.
But also, if time permits, in that time we might try and use up the extra double core, if there is one, in the dark mantle near the LM or do some trenching near the LM.
But that's only if time permits at the very end, depending upon how the consumables run out."
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: They want to call attention to two particular things here.
One, since you guys really haven't gotten any very big rocks so far, they're recommending, they say here, and I quote: "The value of large individual samples has been demonstrated.
We recommend that several football-sized samples of a uniform igneous rock be collected at Station 9 or 10." I'll pass that on as that.
"Another point of interest is the 1- to 20-millimeter size section of the regolith, the dark mantle, the lithology.
Then, any observations or collections you can make pertinent to that would be of interest in trying to determine the relationship of the dark mantle to the subfloor units - the gabbro - underneath."
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Two short questions which I'll ask, which I hope you can answer in just a very few words.
One of them is a yes-and-no answer.
One, they can't find the geophone photos specifically called out in the transcript.
There is apparently a little bit of garble at that point, and the people in the Backroom will be very happy if you could say once and for all, Jack, that, yes, you did get the geophone photos.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: And the second one concerns the 1/4-pound charge which we deployed on the way in last night.
It appears to us from your voice transcript - we weren't fast enough on it at the time - that that may be deployed closer to the ALSEP than the one you deployed on the way out.
And we'd like an impression on that.
And, number 2, you mentioned that you placed it in a depression.
We'd like some feeling for that depression in terms of how much of a danger that bomb...(correcting himself) "charge" might play to the ALSEP when it goes off.
If it's in a depression of any sort, that's probably pretty well protecting the ALSEP.
Any comment on those two questions?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Well, (on) the second one, it's not in a major depression.
But it is maybe a...It's a little dish, maybe a third of a meter deep.
I imagine it will help a little bit.
(Pause) I'm not sure we understand your first question very much.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We have a feeling that when you...
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Bob, don't you have the mileages?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: But there's again some confusion on that.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Yeah, and those mileages also seem to indicate that...We had that callout; remember, you drove back by and you said you saw the flag (on the charge deployed at the star of the outbound drive), and then you said you actually saw the charge itself first.
And it was some time after that you said you deployed the charge.
And we have the opinion from both that and the mileage that you probably deployed the second charge closer to the ALSEP than the first one.
Do you have any sort of a feel for that?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Skeptical) Ah, yeah; I remember saying that.
But that's when I did a big 360, and Jack was out of film; and I just lined up to take that picture with the LM in the background.
And when I said, "Hey, I saw the charge first," I was really...Don't take that comment too strong...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: ...as far as the position of it.
Bob, we're looking for them out there now, as a matter of fact.
We can't see them from here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We'll let it go at that.
And that's all the questions and comments we have on today's traverse.
We'll have a few real-time things on the surface, which I won't bother you with (now).
A possible fix to the Surface Electrical Properties (flapping dust cover) and a possible trip back to the surface gravimeter, which is still having its problems.
But I'll talk with you guys in real-time on those when you get on the surface, rather than bothering you with them now.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: How far should that last charge be from the ALSEP?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: They want it about 300 to 400 meters.
(Long Pause) And, Gene, you gave 0.2 for range (to the SEP transmitter) when...
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: And I guess the question would be: Did you ever go through zero on the way back to the LM?
If you were at 0.2, and we think 092 was the bearing, then the LM is right where it thought it was, and we were just a little confused by our distances.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: No, I don't think I ever went through zero, because I initiated at the SEP.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And, nah, I didn't go through zero.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: This is something to think about.
It's not that far out there.
You know, if there is any question about that thing damaging the ALSEP...It's just hard for us to recall how close they were.
And we sort of thought you had them pinpointed for us.
But, if you want it 3 to 400 meters, you might think about a late drive out there, just to make sure (garbled under Bob) ALSEP.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We don't want to do that.
No, we don't want to do that.
So we'll take care of it.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I can see the charge with the binocular (means "monocular").
It's out almost behind a rock that's between us and the LM, but I can see it.
(Chuckles) I mean, a rock between it and the LM.
I can't give you any idea, though, how far it is.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (To Jack) No, it's the one off to the left.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let me say again, I think we ought to emphasize the exotic looking fragments on the dark mantle.
And we ought to try to make sure that we look at a variety of rocks from the North Massif.
I think we saw the major rock types on the South Massif yesterday, but we really didn't spend a lot of time ranging along the front there to verify that completely.
The other comment (is) on the 1- to 20-millimeter size fraction.
There isn't an awful lot of that in the dark mantle.
That's one of the striking things about it.
In that size range there just isn't very much except chips of what appear to be - (based on) a comparable albedo, anyway, - of the subfloor gabbro.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: But we'll keep our eyes open.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: ...I'll talk with the Backroom about Stations 6 and 7.
We'll get with you on that when you get there.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I was Biomed Right there for about 10 minutes, in case you're curious.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I've got them both (that is, the charges west of the ALSEP).
And the last one we deployed, which I think is the easternmost one (correct), is definitely further out than the first one we deployed (incorrect).
And, you know, to judge distance is awful hard, but looking at Jack's geophones...(To Jack) What's your...(Pause) (To Bob) I got to give you at least 300 meters, Bob.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'm sure they're listening, and we got that.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Yeah, I've got both of them with the monocular now.
And the second one, the last one we deployed, is quite a bit farther out than the first one.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I think that's what they want to hear.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Gordo, I guess it's half again or maybe even twice as far away as the first one we deployed.
So we're going to forget it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And, Gordo, I'm going off the air also here for about 10 minutes.
It'll speed things up a little bit.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: This is the LMP from Challenger.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Jack, you are loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I'm ready for battery management, and the ED Batts are 37.2 (volts).
And I'm going to Power Amp, Primary, and TM (Telemetry), High (bit rate).
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We have high bit rate now.
You're Go on the battery management.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: We're ready for Power Amp, Off, and PCM (Pulse Code Modulation), Low (bit rate).
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I'm sorry, Bob, (for not having noticed the change in CapCom).
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Bob, CDR is starting on with the PLSS donning.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And we copy the forward hatch unlocked, right?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: We've lost downlink from you guys.
We've got a very weak signal.
You might check your configuration up there, please.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: We have you back loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: It looks like I might have hit the Yaw knob on the steerable (antenna) with the PLSS.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And, Jack, if you want to check those again for (steerable antenna pointing) numbers, it's Pitch of one-four and Yaw of eight.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Bob, that doesn't jibe with what my needles say.
I've got a two-zero and five-zero.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: What's your Signal Strength meter reading there on your high gain, next time you get around to it?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: It's not quite as good as it was.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Ed (Fendell) thinks maybe you're on a sidelobe, and he's suggesting a Pitch of fourteen - one-four - and a Yaw of plus eight.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Static clears) Bob, I'm in Auto right now, and it's holding at 3.8 with those numbers that I gave you.
I can't...If I go to the numbers that Ed suggests, I get down to about point three (0.3) and it won't lock up in Auto.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Let's just leave her there, please.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (To Jack) VOX Sens(itivity setting) at Max.
A, T/R; and B is Receive.
On (circuit breaker panel) 16, your breaker open and connect to
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (On) your PLSS comm, Audio breaker, Closed.
Okay, PLSS PTT Main right, verify, and go Mode (switch) A.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You'll get the tones (on), vent flag, press flag, and O flag.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Call Houston and give them your O2 reading.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: This is the LMP on Mode A and my oxygen is nine-four (that is, 94 percent).
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Copy that, Jack, you're loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I'm getting a little bit of a squeal on the initiation of my transmission.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I hear that too, but it's loud and clear here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And LMP PLSS data looks good down here.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: There's a press flag and a vent flag.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, I cannot hear Houston but, Houston, this is CDR with 91 percent.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And, LMP, we read the CDR loud and clear.
We have good LMP medical data.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (To Gene) Okay, you're loud and clear and they got good data on me.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, you go B and I'll go A.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, how do you read me?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Houston, this is the LMP in Bravo.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We read the LMP loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Read you loud and clear also, Gene.
And we have good medical data on you, Gene.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay, Houston, how do you read CDR?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Also loud and clear on AR.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay, Jack, Squelch VHF B, full decrease.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: On (circuit breaker panel) 16, our LCG Pump (circuit breaker) is Closed.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: On 16, Cabin Repress (circuit breaker), Closed, verify.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Suit Fan Delta-P, Open, and Suit Fan number 2, Opened.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, (because the fans are off), we should get a (caution) light in about one minute.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You want us to go by the checklist now on the
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, connect your OPS hose, and I'll get you (purge valve) number 211.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let me turn around so I don't...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Keep hooking this water hose here.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, the (purge valve connector) pin is in.
It's closed, you're in Low Flow.
Believe it or not, it still works.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, are you through with it?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I can get down there, if you can't, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'll get it; I can reach it.
(Pause) Okay, let's get your OPS hose.
OPS hose, it's way down here.
(Pause) Now, let's get the dust cover on it.
The OPS hose is going in.
I verify its locked and the lock-lock is in.
Cover's up, and we'll take another look at them.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, we got the Master Alarm.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: But it's in and it's locked and locked, and you're on...Okay, that's high, that's...You're on Low Flow.
Pin is in and everything's locked.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That's probably as good as anything.
I'm not sure it makes any difference.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, you're over and locked; and dust cover...Verified.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Here's the purge valve; and it's number 208, I hope.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Somehow I copied the wrong one.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Well, I didn't remember from yesterday, and that's what I thought Gordy said.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: On Low and the pin's in.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You can turn (the) Descent Water (valve), Off.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And my hand lub(ricant) is all prepared.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Here we go again, let's take a look at it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, going through one more time.
Your electrical connector and it's locked.
And your OPS is locked, covered.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And vertical on the (PGA) Diverter Valve.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, let me take a check (of Jack's connectors).
PLSS exhaust is locked and covered.
Purge valve is locked and Low.
Okay, let me get your helmet here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let's look at one thing here, Geno.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Let me put this up here.
Get it out of the way, because that's half the battle.
What do you want to look at?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Mine is a little stiff, too, it'll come, if you pull.
If need be, I can pull it down for you.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I can get that one up and out of the way.
As soon as I get this (helmet) over your head, you can put your PLSS fan on.
Okay, now, let's watch out for all your paraphernalia (
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Long Pause as Gene gets Jack's helmet on)
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I just want to make sure I get my fingers on this thing, and make sure it's locked.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Get your fan on here in a minute.
Okay, you got your (PLSS) fan on?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, I want to verify it right now.
(Pause as he feels the neckring) It's aligned.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Pause) See if you can feel that rim all the way around.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And, Geno, we don't see your fan on yet.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay, are the engage marks marked?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Why don't you turn that way and let me turn this way?
Okay, Jack, I'm going to turn these lights off.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, Urine Line Heater's going to come off, and the breaker's out.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, I'm ready except for (turning off the) LCG pump.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We can don our gloves now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, right glove's locked and verified.
(Pause) Okay, and the wrist cover on there...On.
Boy, do I need a shave.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Well, I got my left hand.
Now, let's see what I can do with the right hand.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Almost tempted to take those cover gloves off today.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I might take a look at that, too.
I hate to argue with success, but I need that dexterity today.
(Pause) Bob, I don't know if you caught it yesterday, (but) a little interesting facet of the whole EVA-2 exercise was the fact that I've already worn a...(Comm drop out; pause)
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Gene, you dropped out there right in the middle.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay, I hit the VOX switch on my audio panel.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah, when I picked up my glove.
Okay, Bob, the only thing I said - a little point of interest - I wore the
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And copy that you still have you cover gloves on today, right?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I'll tell you, we have become very respectful of the dust.
transcript.speaker: Cernan
